query: What are the requirements and fees required to submit a valid interconnection application for a large generator in ISO New England, based on the latest ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and ISO-NE Tariff Schedule 22 Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (LGIP)?
final_answer:
Based on the ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and Tariff Schedule 22 (Large Generator Interconnection Procedures - LGIP), here are the requirements and fees for submitting a valid interconnection application for a large generator:

## **Prerequisites**

Before submitting an interconnection request, you must:
- Obtain an ISO Customer ID
- Obtain Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance
- Create an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT)

**Source**: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide (https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide/submit-an-interconnection-request)

## **Timing Requirements**

Interconnection requests can **only** be submitted during a **45 calendar day Cluster Request Window**. The next window is expected to open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026.

**Source**: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; LGIP Section 3.4.2

## **Required Application Components**

A valid Interconnection Request must include:

1. **Completed Interconnection Request Form** (Appendix 1 of LGIP) with:
   - Project name and type (new facility, modification, etc.)
   - Type of interconnection service requested:
     - Network Resource (NR) Interconnection Service (energy capability only)
     - Capacity Network Resource (CNR) Interconnection Service (energy and capacity capability)
   - Facility location and requested point of interconnection
   - Generating facility type and fuel type
   - Capacity information (gross and net MW output at various temperatures)
   - Requested commercial operation date
   - Equipment configuration details

2. **Technical Data and Models** - All data included in Appendix 1 attachments must be provided with the Interconnection Request

3. **Customer Information** - Company details, contact information, authorized signature

**Source**: LGIP Appendix 1 (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

## **Site Control Requirements**

### For CNR Interconnection Service:
- **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is **mandatory** with no exceptions

### For NR Interconnection Service:
Either:
- **100% exclusive Site Control** documentation, OR
- In lieu of Site Control (if Regulatory Limitations exist):
  - **$10,000 per MW deposit** (minimum $500,000, maximum $2,000,000) - refundable within cure period
  - Signed affidavit from company officer stating Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations
  - Documentation explaining the source and effects of such Regulatory Limitations

**Source**: LGIP Appendix 1; ISO-NE Technical Notice on Site Control (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)

## **Fees and Deposits**

### 1. **Application Fee**
- Required with Interconnection Request submission
- Specific amount referenced in LGIP but not detailed in publicly available summaries

### 2. **Study Deposit**
- **$250,000** (uniform amount established by ISO-NE under Order No. 2023 compliance)
- This is an independent entity variation from FERC's standard, which varies deposits by facility size

**Source**: ISO-NE Order 2023 Compliance Overview (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)

### 3. **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)**
- Required to participate in the interconnection process
- Can be paid via:
  - **Cash** (electronic funds transfer only)
  - **Letter of Credit** (must conform to ISO-NE approved template)
  - **Surety Bond** (must conform to ISO-NE approved template)

**Letter of Credit Requirements**:
- Must be from ISO-approved "Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers"
- Must include interconnection request number
- Aggregation limits: $100 million per company, $150 million for affiliate group

**Surety Bond Requirements**:
- Must be from US Treasury-listed Surety
- Minimum "A-" long-term rating from A.M. Best, S&P, or Fitch, or "A3" from Moody's
- Must include interconnection request number
- Aggregation limits: $100 million per company, $150 million for affiliate group

**Source**: ISO-NE Commercial Readiness Deposit Instructions and FAQs (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100026/crd_faq.pdf)

### 4. **Interconnection Facilities Study Deposit**
- **Greater of 25% of estimated study cost OR $250,000**
- Applied toward study costs and agreement development
- Any difference between deposit and actual cost is paid by or refunded to customer

**Source**: LGIP Appendix 3 - Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-3-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-facilities-study-agreement.pdf)

## **Validation Process**

- ISO-NE has **5 Business Days** to review the Interconnection Request
- Customer has up to **10 Business Days** to cure any deficiencies
- All cures must be completed before the Cluster Request Window closes
- ISO recommends submitting at least 15 business days before window close

**Source**: ISO-NE Order 2023 FAQ (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/2024_01_18_tc_order2023_new_process_faq_final.pdf)

## **Important Notes**

- This process reflects the **Order No. 2023 reforms** that transitioned ISO-NE from a serial first-come, first-served process to a **first-ready, first-served cluster study process**
- All Interconnection Requests in a cluster are considered equally queued
- The LGIP applies to Large Generating Facilities (typically >20 MW)
- Additional requirements may apply for specific technology types (wind, solar, storage)

**Source**: LGIP Schedule 22 (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf); ISO-NE Order 2023 Key Project page (https://iso-ne.com/committees/key-projects/order-no-2023-key-project)

I F I C A T I O N\n\nProposed Compliance Overview\n\n## Order 2023 - Improvements to Generator Interconnection Procedures and Agreements\n\n##### Project Title: Order No. 2023 Compliance Proposed Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to Effective Date: March 2024\n\n• ISO New England is preparing its compliance with Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“FERC”) Order No. 2023 \n• This presentation provides: – A summary description of the Order – An overview of ISO’s proposed compliance – Anticipated stakeholder schedule \n\n### SUMMARY DESCRIPTION OF ORDER 2023\n\n### Order No. 2023 Development\n\n• On July 28, 2023, FERC issued Order No. 2023, an approximately 1500-page unanimous Final Rule adopting significant reforms to the standard Large Generator Interconnection Procedures (“LGIP”) and Small Generator Interconnection Procedure (“SGIP”) \n• The reforms build on the standardized procedures that FERC established in Order Nos. 2003, 2006 and 845 to address interconnection queue backlogs, improve certainty, and prevent undue discrimination for new technologies \n• Order No. 2023 adopts most of the reforms proposed in the July 5, 2022 Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (“NOPR”) \n\n### The Need for Reform\n\n• In Order No. 2023, FERC affirmed their preliminary findings that the existing standardized interconnection procedures and agreements are insufficient to ensure interconnection to the transmission system in a reliable, efficient, transparent, and timely manner – The growth of new resources seeking to interconnect and the differing characteristics of those resources have created new challenges for the process \n• These new challenges contribute to queue backlogs and uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting, increasing costs for consumers, which, in turn, can create reliability issues, as needed new generating facilities are unable to come online in an efficient and timely manner \n\n### The Need for Reform, continued\n\n• FERC concludes that, absent reforms, the current process will continue to cause queue backlogs, longer development timelines, and increased uncertainty regarding the cost and timing of interconnecting to the transmission system – Accordingly, pursuant to Federal Power Act Section 206, FERC adopted significant, non-regionally tailored, reforms in the standard LGIP and SGIP \n\n### Order No. 2023 Compliance\n\n• FERC will evaluate compliance filings in light of the independent entity variations standard – Permits ISO-NE to seek to preserve certain variations implemented over the years to improve queue management – However, the order requires significant changes to the manner in which ISO-NE administers the interconnection processes, as well as how interconnection customers are expected to participate in those processes \n• Order No. 2023 was published in the Federal Register on September 6, 2023, setting a compliance deadline of December 5, 2023 \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order Transition to First-Ready First-Served Cluster Process\n\n• Requires that transmission providers make several changes to transition to a first-ready, first-served cluster study process, rather than the current serial first-come, first-served study process – Cluster study process – Increased financial commitments for interconnection customers to enter and remain in the process – New mechanisms for interconnection customers to share interconnection study and upgrade costs – Enhanced site control requirements – Requirements for interconnection customers to select a definitive point of interconnection – Commercial readiness deposit requirements – Withdrawal penalties \n\n##### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Increase the Speed of Interconnection Processing\n\n• Eliminates the reasonable efforts standard for completing interconnection studies, and establishes firm study deadlines along with financial penalties on transmission providers that fail to meet them \n• Adopts a uniform approach to Affected System coordination \n\n### Primary Elements of the Order, continued Incorporate Technological Advancements\n\n• Allow co-location of multiple generating facilities behind a single point of interconnection \n• Allow interconnection customers to access surplus interconnection once the original interconnection customer has an executed LGIA (or an unexecuted filed LGIA) \n• Allow interconnection customer-provided operating assumptions in interconnection studies that change the proposed charging behavior of an electric storage resource \n• Incorporate specific alternative transmission technologies for evaluation during the interconnection study process \n• Incorporate modeling and ride-through requirements for non synchronous generating facilities \n\nREVIEW OF ORDER steps.content:
Based on the ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide and Tariff Schedule 22 (Large Generator Interconnection Procedures - LGIP), here are the requirements and fees for submitting a valid interconnection application for a large generator:

## **Prerequisites**

Before submitting an interconnection request, you must:
- Obtain an ISO Customer ID
- Obtain Critical Energy Infrastructure Information (CEII) clearance
- Create an account in the Interconnection Request Tracking Tool (IRTT)

**Source**: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide (https://www.iso-ne.com/participate/applications-status-changes/interconnection-process-guide/submit-an-interconnection-request)

## **Timing Requirements**

Interconnection requests can **only** be submitted during a **45 calendar day Cluster Request Window**. The next window is expected to open from October 5, 2026 to November 19, 2026.

**Source**: ISO-NE Interconnection Process Guide; LGIP Section 3.4.2

## **Required Application Components**

A valid Interconnection Request must include:

1. **Completed Interconnection Request Form** (Appendix 1 of LGIP) with:
   - Project name and type (new facility, modification, etc.)
   - Type of interconnection service requested:
     - Network Resource (NR) Interconnection Service (energy capability only)
     - Capacity Network Resource (CNR) Interconnection Service (energy and capacity capability)
   - Facility location and requested point of interconnection
   - Generating facility type and fuel type
   - Capacity information (gross and net MW output at various temperatures)
   - Requested commercial operation date
   - Equipment configuration details

2. **Technical Data and Models** - All data included in Appendix 1 attachments must be provided with the Interconnection Request

3. **Customer Information** - Company details, contact information, authorized signature

**Source**: LGIP Appendix 1 (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-1-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-request-with-attachments.pdf)

## **Site Control Requirements**

### For CNR Interconnection Service:
- **100% exclusive Site Control** in the Interconnection Customer's name is **mandatory** with no exceptions

### For NR Interconnection Service:
Either:
- **100% exclusive Site Control** documentation, OR
- In lieu of Site Control (if Regulatory Limitations exist):
  - **$10,000 per MW deposit** (minimum $500,000, maximum $2,000,000) - refundable within cure period
  - Signed affidavit from company officer stating Site Control is unobtainable due to Regulatory Limitations
  - Documentation explaining the source and effects of such Regulatory Limitations

**Source**: LGIP Appendix 1; ISO-NE Technical Notice on Site Control (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100024/notice_re_regulatory_limitations_and_site_control_6_2_25.pdf)

## **Fees and Deposits**

### 1. **Application Fee**
- Required with Interconnection Request submission
- Specific amount referenced in LGIP but not detailed in publicly available summaries

### 2. **Study Deposit**
- **$250,000** (uniform amount established by ISO-NE under Order No. 2023 compliance)
- This is an independent entity variation from FERC's standard, which varies deposits by facility size

**Source**: ISO-NE Order 2023 Compliance Overview (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100003/a04a_2023_09_27_tc_order2023_compliance_overview.pdf)

### 3. **Commercial Readiness Deposit (CRD)**
- Required to participate in the interconnection process
- Can be paid via:
  - **Cash** (electronic funds transfer only)
  - **Letter of Credit** (must conform to ISO-NE approved template)
  - **Surety Bond** (must conform to ISO-NE approved template)

**Letter of Credit Requirements**:
- Must be from ISO-approved "Eligible Letter of Credit Issuers"
- Must include interconnection request number
- Aggregation limits: $100 million per company, $150 million for affiliate group

**Surety Bond Requirements**:
- Must be from US Treasury-listed Surety
- Minimum "A-" long-term rating from A.M. Best, S&P, or Fitch, or "A3" from Moody's
- Must include interconnection request number
- Aggregation limits: $100 million per company, $150 million for affiliate group

**Source**: ISO-NE Commercial Readiness Deposit Instructions and FAQs (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100026/crd_faq.pdf)

### 4. **Interconnection Facilities Study Deposit**
- **Greater of 25% of estimated study cost OR $250,000**
- Applied toward study costs and agreement development
- Any difference between deposit and actual cost is paid by or refunded to customer

**Source**: LGIP Appendix 3 - Interconnection Facilities Study Agreement (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100030/oatt-schedule-22-appendix-3-large-generator-lgip-interconnection-facilities-study-agreement.pdf)

## **Validation Process**

- ISO-NE has **5 Business Days** to review the Interconnection Request
- Customer has up to **10 Business Days** to cure any deficiencies
- All cures must be completed before the Cluster Request Window closes
- ISO recommends submitting at least 15 business days before window close

**Source**: ISO-NE Order 2023 FAQ (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/100007/2024_01_18_tc_order2023_new_process_faq_final.pdf)

## **Important Notes**

- This process reflects the **Order No. 2023 reforms** that transitioned ISO-NE from a serial first-come, first-served process to a **first-ready, first-served cluster study process**
- All Interconnection Requests in a cluster are considered equally queued
- The LGIP applies to Large Generating Facilities (typically >20 MW)
- Additional requirements may apply for specific technology types (wind, solar, storage)

**Source**: LGIP Schedule 22 (https://www.iso-ne.com/static-assets/documents/regulatory/tariff/sect_2/sch22/sch_22_lgip.pdf); ISO-NE Order 2023 Key Project page (https://iso-ne.com/committees/key-projects/order-no-2023-key-project)
